Safety Mar 24, 2026

Pilot Shares What It’s Like To Operate A Red-Eye Flight Overnight

These kinds of flights can take a toll on pilots.

Circadian rhythm disruption from red-eye operations creates measurable fatigue risks that extend beyond individual pilot wellness into broader flight safety and crew scheduling protocol considerations.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

This Is How Fast The SR-71 Blackbird Could Fly

The incredible power of the fastest jet in the USAF.

The SR-71's sustained Mach 3.2 cruise capability required exotic titanium alloys and fuel management systems that pioneered thermal engineering solutions still referenced in modern hypersonic aircraft development.

See Simple Flying →

Drones Mar 24, 2026

Drone smuggling scheme ends in 81-year prison sentence

For most drone pilots, a flight ends with a smooth landing and maybe some great footage. For one Florida man, it ended with an 81-year prison sentence. more…

A single drone smuggling operation resulted in an 81-year sentence, signaling federal courts are treating commercial contraband distribution via UAS as severe felonies comparable to major trafficking crimes.

Safety Mar 24, 2026

NTSB reviews runway alerts, staffing and ARFF response at LaGuardia.

NTSB's multi-factor investigation suggests potential systemic gaps in airport infrastructure, staffing levels, or emergency response coordination that may extend beyond this single incident.

Regulatory Mar 24, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS shifts aviation safety culture from merely investigating accidents to systematically identifying and mitigating risks before incidents occur.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA Research Proposes Technology to Seek Earth-Like Exoplanets

As NASA seeks to understand the mysteries of the universe, the agency is advancing technologies to locate and explore Earth-like planets far beyond our solar system. A key element of this research involves observing reflected light from exoplanets, which can reveal indicators of Earth-like featur...

NASA's exoplanet detection technology advancement could drive innovations in aerospace optical systems and remote sensing capabilities with broader terrestrial applications.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA cancels Lunar Gateway space station to focus on Moon base

NASA has effectively canceled the Lunar Gateway space station as the centerpiece of its Artemis architecture, announcing on… The post NASA cancels Lunar Gateway space station to focus on Moon base appeared first on AeroTime.

NASA's pivot away from orbital Gateway toward direct lunar surface infrastructure reduces aerospace contractor opportunities in cislunar logistics but accelerates Moon base development timelines.

Mesa Approves Falcon Field Landing Fees

City leaders say landing fees address an airport funding gap, while flight schools warn of rising costs.

Landing fee increases at flight training hubs risk pricing out cost-conscious flight schools and student pilots during a critical pilot shortage period.

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Organizations must document their rationale for accepting residual risk after implementing controls, creating an auditable trail of safety decision-making that regulators expect under Part 5 requirements.

High-resolution satellite imagery distribution through regional partners expands commercial space data accessibility for Canadian and international markets.

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA’s Water-Hunting Tool Will Help Scout Moon’s South Pole

NASA is joining international partners to hunt for ice on the Moon in support of future human exploration. The agency is providing a water-detecting instrument, the Neutron Spectrometer System (NSS), to the Lunar Polar Exploration (LUPEX) mission led by JAXA (Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency) a...

NASA's neutron spectrometer on Japan's lunar mission will provide critical subsurface ice mapping data essential for establishing sustainable human bases at the Moon's south pole.

See NASA →

Saab signs Cohere AI deal for GlobalEye campaign in Canada

Saab has signed a memorandum of understanding with Canadian artificial intelligence company Cohere to explore AI applications linked… The post Saab signs Cohere AI deal for GlobalEye campaign in Canada appeared first on AeroTime.

Saab's integration of generative AI capabilities into GlobalEye airborne surveillance systems signals a strategic shift toward autonomous data analysis, potentially accelerating intelligence processing for military and government customers.

Corporate Mar 24, 2026

Why In The World Did Boeing Merge With McDonnell Douglas?

A closer look at the strategic and financial reasons behind Boeing’s McDonnell Douglas merger.

Boeing's 1997 merger with McDonnell Douglas shifted the company's focus from engineering-driven culture to financial optimization, establishing priorities that would influence aircraft development decisions for decades.

Safety Mar 24, 2026

Surface Safety Spotlight – Los Angeles International Airport, CA (LAX) – From the Flight Deck

Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) is one of the nation’s busiest commercial airports. This short video highlights a runway incursion hazard where pilots exiting Runway 24R, particularly via taxi

Runway exit geometry at LAX creates predictable pilot confusion during high-traffic periods, making standardized taxiway signage and ground control callouts critical for incursion prevention.

Military Mar 24, 2026

US Army orders 2,500 Skydio drones in record deal

The US Army just made one thing very clear: small drones aren’t just helpful anymore; they’re essential. In a record-setting move, Skydio has secured an order worth more than $52 million to supply over 2,500 of its X10D drones to the Army. That makes it the largest small drone procurement from a ...

The Army's $52M commitment to 2,500 Skydio drones signals that domestic small UAS now meet military operational standards, reshaping defense procurement away from foreign-dependent platforms.

See DroneDJ →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

NASA Unveils Initiatives to Achieve America’s National Space Policy

As part of its “Ignition” event on Tuesday, NASA announced a series of transformative agencywide initiatives designed to achieve President Donald J. Trump’s National Space Policy and advance American leadership in space. These actions reflect the urgency of the moment, but also the tremendous opp...

NASA's alignment with Trump's National Space Policy signals accelerated timelines and potential budget reallocations toward specific space priorities, directly impacting aerospace contractors' competitive positioning.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 24, 2026

SES orders 28 satellites from K2 Space for next-gen MEO network

SES has ordered an initial 28 satellites from manufacturing startup K2 Space for meoSphere, a next-generation MEO network slated to be in operation by 2030. The post SES orders 28 satellites from K2 Space for next-gen MEO network appeared first on SpaceNews.

SES's commitment to K2 Space demonstrates growing reliance on specialized manufacturing startups rather than traditional aerospace giants for satellite constellation buildout.

See SpaceNews →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

This Tiny Airline Just Launched London's New Longest-Ever Airbus A220 Route

Gatwick has launched its longest-ever Airbus A220 route with new Bucharest service.

The A220's extended range capability is enabling smaller carriers to penetrate international long-haul markets previously dominated by larger operators, reshaping competitive dynamics at secondary airports.

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

What Is American Airlines' Longest Route In 2026?

Arguably having much more of a domestic focus, US carrier American Airlines doesn’t quite have the same intercontinental reach as archrival United Airlines. Nonetheless, the oneworld alliance member still manages to fly pretty far from home, serving cities like Delhi, Sydney, and Auckland. But wh...

American Airlines' longest 2026 route reflects strategic positioning within oneworld alliance partnerships rather than independent long-haul capacity compared to United.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 24, 2026

YFQ-44A Fury CCA is Now in Production

Anduril announced the start of the YFQ-44A’s production at its Arsenal-1 factory, with new manufacturing processes allowing to supply a large number of drones to the U.S. Air Force. Anduril’s YFQ-44A Fury Collaborative Combat Aircraft (CCA) has entered serial production at the firm’s much touted ...

Anduril's Arsenal-1 factory is implementing novel manufacturing processes to achieve high-volume production of the Fury CCA, significantly accelerating autonomous combat aircraft delivery timelines to the Air Force.

Industry Mar 24, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Optimizing existing operations through technology integration offers immediate environmental gains while capacity expansions require coordinated airline-airport-airspace improvements to handle projected traffic growth.

See MDPI →

Drones Mar 24, 2026

DJI’s latest deals make shaky phone footage a thing of the past

Smartphones today can shoot cinematic 4K, handle low light, and even mimic DSLR depth. But there’s still one thing they can’t fix on their own: movement. That tiny shake when you walk. That awkward jitter when you pan. That “almost good” clip that never quite looks professional. That’s exactly wh...

DJI's stabilization technology is expanding smartphone cinematography capabilities beyond traditional gimbal solutions, creating new commercial opportunities for mobile content creators.

See DroneDJ →

Commercial Mar 24, 2026

Why Airlines Are Retiring the 757

Few aircraft in commercial aviation have earned the kind of reputation the Boeing 757 built over the years. It was powerful, versatile, and capable of flying routes that many narrowbody aircraft simply couldn’t handle. Airlines loved it because it could operate from short runways, climb quickly t...

The 757's retirement reflects airlines' shift toward modern fuel efficiency and commonality with larger fleets rather than capability limitations, as its unique short-haul range advantage becomes less valuable in optimized networks.

Fragmented sensor data across government agencies represents an untapped resource that could significantly enhance missile defense integration and situational awareness.

Finnair's pivot to Embraer E2s over Airbus demonstrates that total cost of ownership and fleet flexibility now outweigh traditional OEM loyalty in carrier aircraft selection.
